title: The Dungeon Dive's Top 50 Games of All Time* - part 4
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=i_bfUG57Je8

The penultimate ten, and the list starts shedding doubts. Daniel (@dungeondive) opens by conceding that **Tomb** at #42 was probably a mistake — kept on potential rather than performance — before climbing into the higher slots.

**Eldritch Horror** at 20, with the Forsaken Lore and Strange Remnants small-box expansions, has now overtaken Arkham Horror 3rd in his rotation purely on its sandboxy globe-trotting feel; the board is just way too big for small tables. **Wander: The Cult of Barnacle Bay** (19) is the surprise of the run, beating Massive Darkness for Daniel on the strength of a small encounter deck and an elegant initiative track. **Xia: Legends of a Drift System** (18, with Embers of a Forsaken Star) channels space-opera anime. **D100 Dungeon** (17) with the World Builder and Mapping Game is a hybrid solo-RPG/board-game that rewards player imagination.

**Dark Light: Memento Mori** (16) is the glorious mess Daniel will never sell — flawed, unbalanced, and beloved. **Cthulhu: Death May Die** (15) — fast, frantic, furious, the highest-ranked CMON title. **Mansions of Madness 2nd Ed** (14) is his go-to onboarding game for new hobbyists. **Dungeon Degenerates** (13) gets the love letter for its outsider-art ethos and intersectionality with broader folk-art and punk culture. **Quest for the Lost Pixel** (12) — the unofficial game of the channel. **Kingdom Death: Monster** (11) closes on Dark Souls / Berserk / Monster Hunter as a survival-horror masterpiece walled off by price.

----

Daniel calls Dungeon Degenerates the game that recentred his reviewing — looking at games as folk art and outsider art rather than in a vacuum. Has a single game ever changed the lens you bring to the hobby?
